perturbation.original_text: Co-Host will use commercially reasonable efforts to market the Products it orders to the best of its ability, and to that end will (i) conduct marketing activities authorized by NAI, (ii) support special promotions initiated by NAI, and (iii) maintain a sound financial condition. Co-Host will conduct its business in a manner that reflects favorably upon the Products and NAI.
perturbation.changed_text: Co-Host will endeavor to market the Products it orders in a manner it deems appropriate, and to that end may (i) participate in some marketing activities suggested by NAI, (ii) consider supporting special promotions initiated by NAI, and (iii) attempt to maintain a reasonably sound financial condition. Co-Host will try to conduct its business in a manner that does not significantly harm the reputation of the Products and NAI.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has clear obligations, while the changed text introduces vagueness. 'Commercially reasonable efforts' becomes 'endeavor to market in a manner it deems appropriate,' making the marketing effort subjective. 'Will conduct' becomes 'will try to conduct', and removing 'maintaining a sound financial condition' creates ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17200 prohibits unfair competition, including unfair or deceptive business practices. By weakening the marketing obligations and financial responsibility of Co-Host, the modified text increases the potential for Co-Host to engage in practices that could be considered unfair or deceptive, or to fail to maintain financial stability, which can affect the fulfillment of consumer orders, directly contradicting this law's aim to ensure fair business practices.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken the original obligations, potentially leading to unfair business practices, as described in the provided law explanation. Specifically, California Business and Professions Code § 17200, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, defines unfair competition as 'any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice.' The original text had clearer requirements for marketing and financial condition, while the modified text uses subjective terms and weakens the obligations. This increases the potential for practices that could be considered unfair, thus contradicting the law's aim to ensure fair business practices by creating vague language that is unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise agreed upon in writing by NAI, Co-Host will submit each advertisement and promotion to NAI for trademark review and approval prior to initial release, which approval will not be unreasonably delayed or withheld. All such usage which was not expressly approved by NAI must be terminated immediately upon receipt of notice from NAI to that effect.
perturbation.changed_text: Co-Host will generally aim to submit advertisements and promotions to NAI for trademark review when practical. NAI's approval process will be handled in a generally timely manner. Usage not explicitly approved by NAI should ideally be discontinued following notification from NAI.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has clear directives and timelines for trademark review and compliance, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity by using terms like 'generally aim,' 'when practical,' 'generally timely manner,' and 'should ideally be discontinued.' This weakens the obligation to obtain approval and the consequences of non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act, specifically 15 U.S.C. § 1114, protects trademark owners from unauthorized use of their trademarks that is likely to cause confusion, mistake, or deception. By making the trademark review process optional and less stringent, the modified text increases the risk of Co-Host using NAI's trademarks in a way that could infringe on NAI's rights and confuse consumers. The original text ensures stricter adherence to trademark guidelines, while the modified text weakens these protections and creates a risk of trademark infringement that the Lanham Act aims to prevent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Lanham Act (15 U.S.C. § 1114) by weakening the trademark review process, potentially leading to unauthorized use of NAI's trademarks. The original text mandates trademark review and approval, whereas the changed text uses ambiguous language like 'generally aim' and 'when practical,' which reduces the obligation for Co-Host to obtain approval. Snippet 1 and 2 of 15 U.S.C. § 1114 state that any person who uses "any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive;...shall be liable in a civil action." By making the trademark review process less stringent, the changed text increases the risk of trademark infringement, a direct violation of the Lanham Act's protections against unauthorized trademark use that could cause confusion.

perturbation.original_text: Co-Host shall not have the right to assign or otherwise transfer this Agreement or any rights herein granted to any other person or entity, except by operation of law or in connection with the sale of all of its assets, or the acquisition of the Co-Host by a third party. Any such attempted assignment shall be void and the Agreement shall remain in effect.
perturbation.changed_text: Co-Host will generally not assign or transfer this Agreement without considering the implications and following standard business practices, except possibly in cases involving legal mandates or significant company restructuring. Any potential assignment might be subject to review, and the Agreement may continue to apply pending further evaluation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly prohibits assignment except in specific circumstances and states that unauthorized attempts are void. The modified text uses vague language like 'generally not assign,' 'considering the implications,' 'possibly in cases involving legal mandates,' and 'might be subject to review,' creating ambiguity around the prohibition and the consequences of attempted assignment.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1457 states that the burden of an obligation may be transferred with the consent of the party entitled to its benefit, but not otherwise, except as provided by Section 1466. The original text adheres to this by explicitly prohibiting assignment except in specific circumstances. The modified text introduces ambiguity, making the prohibition less clear, which could lead to attempted assignments without proper consent, contradicting the clarity required by CA Civil Code § 1457. This is because the conditions when an assignment is valid becomes unclear.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1457 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 1. OBLIGATIONS IN GENERAL [1427 - 1543] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. TRANSFER OF OBLIGATIONS [1457 - 1471] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1457. The burden of an obligation may be transferred with the consent of the party entitled to its benefit, but not otherwise, except as provided by Section 1466. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly prohibits assignment except under specific conditions, aligning with California Civil Code § 1457, which states that the burden of an obligation can only be transferred with the consent of the benefiting party unless otherwise provided. The modified text introduces ambiguity around this prohibition, using terms like 'generally not assign' and 'possibly in cases.' This creates uncertainty about when assignment is permissible and weakens the requirement for explicit consent as mandated by CIV 1457. The 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' definition states that a legal contradiction occurs when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law.
